data.title: General Motors Q3 Earnings Preview: It's A Make-Or-Break Moment
data.description:
General Motors Goes Countertrend

Who says automakers were not the right place to invest in 2024? Well, while most of the major automakers have deeply underperformed the market, General Motors Company (NYSE:GM) has not only been able to gain positive returns, but it has also outperformed the market, not counting dividends. As for me, my readers know that I picked Stellantis N.V. (STLA) two years ago, and while up to Q1 of this year, it turned out to be a market-trouncing investment, returning over 2x, since then, it has been nothing but woes and it is now the worst performing stock in the industry.

As General Motors' Q3 earnings approach, it is time to understand why it performed so well this year and its prospects as we get close to year-end and FY 2025. After all, GM had been an underperformer until this year. As concerns over the automotive industry mount, we should be cautious in assessing whether GM can still stand out as a market-beating pick or if the good times are already behind us.

In particular, General Motors was able to beat the market thanks to higher-than-expected profits ($10.1B in FY2023 and $11.2B as the top-end of the FY2024 guidance) and a well-managed cost-reduction program with an important $2B reduction in its fixed costs net of D&A. Free cash flow generated in FY2023 was $11.7B. The company was thus able to return $12B to its shareholders via dividends and generous buybacks.

We see that General Motors invested and focused on reducing the overall part count, with an 80% reduction in buildable combinations, a 60% reduction in selectable options, and 35% fewer trims. This created a total reduction of over 1,000 parts.

An encouraging piece of data for GM is its market share in the U.S., which has been constantly growing in the past few quarters. Moreover, GM's share in the EV market is rapidly increasing. This is helping GM's EV business achieve long-sought profitability, which should be reached by Q4. Good news recently came from an unexpected report showing that sales in China rose 14.3% QoQ with EV and PHEV sales surpassing ICE vehicles (52.7% of total sales).

With these fundamentals - leaner operations and growing market share - it is understandable that a profitable company expects a growing EBIT and FCF even in a tough industry year like this one.

It also seems GM is feasting on Stellantis' market share decline in the U.S. Just a few years ago, Stellantis reported a double-digit market share in the U.S., thanks to the strength of American brands such as Jeep, Ram, and Chrysler. But since 2022, Stellantis has lost market share and is now fighting for 8%. At the same time, GM reported a market share of 15% in Q1 2024 and 15.9% in Q2 2024. The recent Q3 sales news release told us that worldwide, GM increased its deliveries by 2%, but in the U.S. volume was down 2%. However, GM reported record EV sales (up 60% YoY), saying it will reach the breakeven point by the end of the year. This makes GM the second-largest EV player in the U.S., beating Stellantis. Surely, GM's EV portfolio has gained appeal among U.S. consumers, with over 50% of EV buyers being new to GM. This is positive, but we will have to watch what the impact of the 2% decline in U.S. sales will bring. Has GM lost market share? Recent estimates believe the car market in the U.S. grew by 0.2% in the third quarter. This would mean GM lost a bit of market share. But we will have to see what it did versus its main competitors such as Stellantis, Ford, Toyota, and Volkswagen. Surely, GM has been able to follow a correct pricing strategy, that has not aimed at double-digit margins in the way Stellantis did but has still been able to generate profits for the company, while offering models whose value was perceived by the customers. This is why GM didn't need to heavily discount its models, at least so far. Stellantis, on the other hand, has truly and rapidly turned into the Cinderella of today's market with a 20% YoY decline in shipments. However, thanks to heavy discounts, Stellantis' market share in the U.S. climbed from 7.2% in July, to 7.9% in August, to 8% in September while inventory was down 11.6%. While this may be positive in terms of market share, we should not be fooled: Stellantis' bottom line at the end of Q3 won't look pretty because North America was Stellantis' most profitable market. Heavy discounts to clear inventory will generate a bleeding income statement.

GM Q3 Earnings Preview

We have seen how GM represents a true exception in the automotive world. Yet, we should not be overconfident as the industry certainly has a tough road ahead. Limited growth, margin compression, credit deterioration, and lower ASPs expected to go on through 2025 are all factors looming on the horizon and partly already present. GM will be affected by this environment, but it enters this challenging period in good shape. In particular, it seems to have implemented a correct pricing strategy where it accepted lower margins vs. its peers in 2022 and 2023 to focus on leaner and nimbler production to cope better once the "value-over-volume" strategy would have turned unsustainable for most OEMs.

Let's now make some educated guesses on what GM's Q3 earnings may look like.

Firstly, GM was bold and confident enough to raise its FY guidance twice in the past two quarters. If the company can maintain it, then we should see a solid report. We are talking about an industrial FCF that should be above $10B and EBIT margins in the high single digits. If GM achieves these results, it will probably surpass Stellantis in profitability. What matters is that GM should report an industrial FCF of around $20B by the end of the year, while Stellantis will probably report an outflow of $10B. The gap stands out as a sentence.

Current consensus expects GM's EPS to grow 7.4% to $2.45 while GM's revenues should come in rather flat (consensus: -0.5%). Besides a focus on cost management, GM's EPS will benefit from the massive buyback program the company is carrying out. In June, the company's board authorized a repurchase of up to $6B that summed up with a $10B accelerated share repurchase program approved in November 2023. Of this program, $1.4B was remaining as of June and GM exhausted this authorization by the end of Q2, reducing its share count by 18% in just one year. Yes, you read that right, 18%. This is the yield received through the buyback, which can then be added to the dividend. So, we should also look at GM's share repurchases in Q3 to see how much of the newly authorized program is still being carried out. After all, the stock has surely benefited from these share repurchases as it has gathered popularity among investors and has soared to its 52-week high of just below $50. The target for GM is to have a share count below 1B. At the end of Q2, it reported 1.1B shares, so there were still at least 100M shares to be bought back by GM. Considering an average share price of around $45 in Q3, GM should have spent around $4.5B to reach its goal, leaving at least another $1.5B under the remaining authorization. My guess is that it probably didn't spend this much and that we should see at least $3B left under the current authorization.

The outlook for FY2024 is $178B in revenue (+3.8% YoY) and EPS of $9.97 (almost 30% YoY growth). I believe that we should not be superficial as we read GM's Q3 report. As much as the company has been able to perform quite well, the environment is so difficult that we should be weary and watchful for any possible sign of excessive weakness or lower profits. In fact, GM currently trades at a fwd PE of 4.8. It may seem cheap, but it is a rather fair multiple for a large OEM. But if GM's earnings don't grow as fast as expected, we would immediately have a multiple above 5 and even close to 6. In this market, it would almost certainly trigger a sell-off to reposition the stock between a 4 and 5 fwd PE. This is exactly what happened to Stellantis, which currently trades at a fwd PE of 4.9. The stock is falling accordingly to the downward earnings revisions.

Now, on this ground, I consider GM stock a hold currently. I see a financially strong company that will probably end this fiscal year with nice profits. However, its current valuation, as cheap as it may seem, positions the company in the high range of its usual multiple. A little bit of softness may thus cause GM's stock to tumble as well since investors are, for the most part, nervous about the industry. I have a small stake in GM which I never increased to a full position due to the deteriorating environment. I am neither increasing before earnings nor am I selling. I believe the company has enough strength to support its stock and keep performing well in its key markets, but I am a bit in doubt regarding the possibility of reporting very strong results in a deteriorating environment where consumers are pulling back or postponing their next car purchase. Truly, we are before a make-or-break moment. We will either see GM remark on its regained strength or we could see it aligning with the overall industry. Depending on the outcome, the stock could take two very different paths.

data.title: Uniper Stock: 'Hold' - Very Few Positive Catalysts
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I mean to update you on Uniper (OTCPK:UNPRF), a utility I have covered in the past but never actually bought. My last article, if you're interested, can be found here. Since that article, the RoR we've seen from Uniper is as follows, and confirms my overall stance for the business.

To be clear, calling Uniper a bad business, despite Fortum's (OTCPK:FOJCF) exit, is not accurate. It's actually a fairly good business. The problem is that the company has non-trivial challenges to face both now and in the future, coupled with a valuation that juxtaposed to the company's challenges is not something you can expect "working out" unless you significantly put a premium to the company's future cash flows.

Given what else is available on the utility market, I don't see much possibility for this, and I would view such a trend or such a position as excessive. Previously, I maintained my neutral rating at "Hold", despite the company in a short time dropping 50% - but as you can see above, the company has actually continued to drop down in a material way since my last coverage. The valuation drop does mean calling the company a "Sell" here is not really justified - but at the same time, have we reached the sort of valuation that would entail us to be able to enter this investment? That's one of the questions I will aim to answer in this article.

Uniper - The company's valuation is high, but the company's appeal is even potentially higher if certain things happen

There could be an upside to Uniper - I want to be clear about that. My "job" in this case is really making clear at what price I view such an upside as justified. The Interim results for 2024 1H24 are the latest ones we have at this time, presented back in August. The new results aren't coming until 2-3 weeks in the future - I can give a preview of them, but that's about it.

Positives first. A substantially improved performance seen to the company's expectations enables Uniper to actually increase its overall outlook for results. The company has been spending a lot of money on growth CapEx for specific projects. Uniper, despite everything, is also on solid financial ground as I see it. Another major positive that warrants mentioning here is that the company has derisked the natgas and export portfolio, with the company's termination of Gazprom's long-term contracts. This is yet another positive in context, even if I can believe and expect the company once (and if) Russia comes back to the board, to restore some of these things.

1H24 operational results were better than expected, with a net income of $1.113B on an adjusted basis, with adjusted EBITDA up to €1.74B for the half-year. This means that the company's full-year outlook has been raised to upwards of €2.4B in adjusted EBITDA, with adjusted net income upwards of €1.5B. The exact nature of these figures will depend on the payment obligations to the German state. The annual results will dictate what the company has to do with regard to that.

But in finance and risk, a lot has happened for Uniper. The company now has economic net cash of over €6B as of the middle of the year. Uniper's IG rating has also been confirmed at a BBB- from S&P and Scope, and this marks a rise from the previous period.

Overall, the financial circumstances necessary to drive the company's plans are now in effect and "there".

The company's aforementioned de-risking is another positive. An arbitration panel has stated that €13B will be paid by Gazprom export in damages to Uniper, and this payment will in effect be directly transferred to the German state for the associated bailout of Uniper back when it was necessary.

Other than that, both disposals and sales in the company, as well as its various strategic targets, are underway.

This also includes the company's new investment and growth CapEx projects, including a very significant investment in a hydropower plant - PSP Happurg.

As you can see, the company plans to bring this plant back to life in the next few years. I have covered with other utilities that when it comes to investment projects and new builds and projects, the returns are currently not favorable. When I say that, I mean that based on the hedged potential power price of around €45/MWh, most projects or builds, such as solar, onshore or offshore wind, or nuclear, do not make a lot of fiscal sense. Projects like this though, where the company can clearly expect good returns above WACC, are a different story - and frankly, Uniper needs these.

Overall, I will state with clarity that Uniper has improved both its financial and fundamental position as of 1H24. This is undisputed. This is based not only on soft targets but also hard requirements for a return to the capital markets, which has been Uniper's goal for some time. The company's balance sheet restructuring is finished, and the company has the financial ability to pay a regular, not-unattractive dividend. The fact that the company cannot currently legally pay their shareholders a dividend, is a different story.

But we also have updated financial KPI's and increased transparency, which brings Uniper more in line with other European utilities. The company has successfully managed to secure external, independent financing again, and has re-established its debt issuance program successfully. Once again, there's a syndicated credit line. All these things, in conjunction with the improved credit rating, and the termination of Export contracts for Gazprom, are steps in the right direction.

The company's performance looks bad, but needs to be taken in clear context to both where the company was, and where it is going (and also what sort of comps we had at the time, which I have been covering in previous articles on Uniper).

The company needs to achieve a normalized level of good earnings, which would enable shareholder visibility in terms of earnings and dividends. In order to do so, the company still has ways to go. The company's flexible generation is on the path to normalized earnings. The decline is related to lower-one-off earnings because the procurement of Russian Replacement gas volumes is still not entirely done.

With the outlook raised due to good KPIs and good hedging, the company can now expect a far better (by which I mean €400M above original expectations) 2024E result. Especially Gas midstream, which the company is still "building up" did far better than expected, as well as some positive net interest results. This is despite some fairly volatile electricity and gas prices.

It is also despite the company's carbon emissions being down 11% since I last reviewed the company. Managing this result while at the same time lowering in this fashion is not a bad performance - the same is true while also increasing its production volume, and green commodities filling in the form of gas storage, which at 1H24 for the winter period is at 85% filled. Like Fortum, Uniper has hedged over 80% of 2024, and over 50% of 2025, and has already hedged for 2026 - though the hedges on a forward basis are somewhat lower than the contracts that Fortum has managed.

The company is now using a three-prong/segment approach to its utility business. Green generation, in the form of Nuclear and Hydro and renewables, Flex generation, which is a nice name for Gas and what remains of the coal-fired generation, and Greener Commodities, which contains the company's gas midstream segment.

On a high level, this is an attractive business - we just need the right entry to get the sort of results or return possibilities that we want.

Valuation for Uniper - not great, but it's getting better

Remember, in my last article I gave the company a PT of around €20/share. First question, am I sticking to this PT? UN0 is the main ticker here, and while we've seen improvements, I still don't see forecasts at this time where a €40-€45/share PT would be justified. To give you a picture of what the company's earnings estimates look like at this time, we have the following.

When you look at things like this, you may start to understand the underlying reasoning behind my price targets and my expectations for Uniper. Utilities and their earnings are fairly tied to spot prices, commodity prices, and the like. While it is of course possible that we'll see spikes in this and corresponding spikes in Uniper's results, it is not something I want to base my thesis on. The company's decline in earnings you see above in 2024-2025E is based on a few factors, in part things like the shutdown of coal-fired generation, which still represents a fairly substantial part of the company's mix.

It's my conviction, that based on this, we cannot expect this company to significantly improve or outperform in a way that would clearly justify the sort of price that the company currently demands. Despite improvements, I would much rather at this time have my money in a money market fund at 3.9%, which is my current yield on cash (though I expect this to drop in the near term).

This is a risk/reward consideration. Despite improvements, I do not yet see a scenario where I can see a clear upside from this price, and for that reason, I am also not improving or raising my price target level at this time.

My thesis for Uniper as of October 2024 is as follows.

Thesis

  • Uniper, a European class-leading company in legacy fossil fuel generation, has gone through very hard times. With a market cap of over €20B, it's a solid play at the right valuation, but this "play" also requires you to have a very long-term outlook for the business. Because any upside investors can see here, is likely to be longer-term incoming. I would only buy this company at an extremely cheap price.
  • If we consider Uniper at a 16-18x P/E, the company's upside is nearly 20% annualized until 2026E, estimating a PT of €60-70. However, this would be an extremely exuberant valuation and I do not believe it is likely.
  • I consider Uniper to be interesting only at a valuation of below €20/share, as the risk-reward stands today. This means that I am currently on a "HOLD", and I do not change my thesis here as of October of 2024. I continue to watch, but I'm not going in here.

Remember, I'm all about:

1. Buying undervalued - even if that undervaluation is slight, and not mind-numbingly massive - companies at a discount, allowing them to normalize over time and harvesting capital gains and dividends in the meantime.

2. If the company goes well beyond normalization and goes into overvaluation, I harvest gains and rotate my position into other undervalued stocks, repeating #1.

3. If the company doesn't go into overvaluation, but hovers within a fair value, or goes back down to undervaluation, I buy more as time allows.

4. I reinvest proceeds from dividends, savings from work, or other cash inflows as specified in #1.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills only 2 out of my 5 investment criteria, making it a "HOLD" here. I do not see a near-term scenario where this company becomes an attractive play.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Swedbank: Uncertain Given Interest Rate Trends, But Upside Is Possible
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Swedbank AB (publ) (OTCPK:SWDBF) (OTCPK:SWDBY) is a company that I have been following for years. It is, in fact, the first company I ever wrote about on Seeking Alpha way back when. In my last article, which was published in June of this year, or 4 months ago at this point, I changed my rating to a "Hold". This was the right choice.

Why was this the right choice?

Because the company has underperformed quite clearly since that time.

So with that in mind, both my "Buy" rating and my change to a "Hold" was the right way to go. Swedbank is primarily an income investment - one that I combine with valuation to see an outperformance over time, and I have done this successfully for some time - years now in fact. As it stands now, I remain long Swedbank - but I have not bought shares in the company since 2023 when I last was a "Buy" on the company, and at a better price than the article at the time suggested, since there is a slight lag on publishing and buying in this case.

So in this article, I mean to look at 2Q24, preview 3Q24, and see what Swedbank can give you. When it comes to any bank or financial institution with exposure to NII at this time, we need to understand the impacts of the high interest rates we've had, and also where it goes from here.

Let's look at recent results and trends.

Swedbank - The upside is there, but it is lower than we might expect

Swedbank is an interesting bank. When I started writing about it over 5 years ago, we were in the midst of a brewing scandal regarding AML/KYC that drove the company down into the double-digit native share price levels.

Obviously, we can only dream of such a level at this time, when the company trades at upward of 200+ SEK for the native ticker SWED-A. For the new quarter, several trends bear mentioning here. First off, inflation. Since Swedbank exposure is Scandinavia and Sweden, the inflation here is paramount - and it's been quite severe for some time - but is now lower. GDP growth is unfortunately still down. It was negative in Sweden during 2023, it's expected to be at 0.1% this year, and not to recover until 2025E, at which point we're talking about 2.7%. In short, the outlook for inflation and GDP growth in the coming year is a positive one.

As is written here, it's also my estimate that we're going to look at higher interest rates for longer, which is one of the reasons I've gone and tied down some of my loans at close to 3% - albeit for a short time.

Income and fiscal trends for Swedbank during 2Q24. These were positive. C/I is one of the lowest in all of Europe because we're talking about around 35%, with a RoE of 17.5% and a quarterly EPS of over 7.6 SEK, annualizing to over 28 SEK for the full year. This is despite a 3% reduction in NII as some early cuts in interest rates start making themselves felt across the company's P&Ls. Net Commission income is weighing things up slightly, and overall the total pre-impairment profit was -1%, but due to some tax benefits, the net profit for Swedbank was up 2% YoY.

So, a positive.

Swedbank remains not as exposed to private-line mortgages as Handelsbanken. This is a positive. The company has solid credit recoveries, a strong liquidity position, a CET-1 capital buffer of 5 pp in excess of requirement, and it now holds very strong scores in European crime prevention. The company's risks are primarily relegated to the Baltic operations that Swedbank has, which also happen to be the company's riskier areas and exposures.

On the private side, things are looking up - increases in customers, and good volumes ensure this. On the corporate side, the company has initiated a new partnership in Finland with Aktia, as well as expanded its leadership in corporate lending in many of the nations here, but above all Lithuania. Lending volumes for the group are up 13% excluding FX year-over-year, and despite inflation, interest rates, and the like, are still up QoQ, and even more in the Baltics than in Sweden.

Deposits also continue to grow - 1.3% QoQ in Sweden and more in the Baltics. Where we find issues is the aforementioned NII segment. Despite higher average lending and deposit volumes, market rates are starting to impact returns - and this is only the beginning. You will see this be a recurring trend across this article, that the market rate returns dictate only one direction for the company's trajectory in the next 2-3 years, and that is down.

This is despite increased wholesale funding. Any upside from lending and deposit volumes is offset by the nearly 600M SEK quarterly margin/treasury impact that the company has here.

Net commission income is an upside here, and worth mentioning. Asset management was up, and cards were higher than seasonally typical, leading to an almost 5% QoQ development in NCI.

Swedbank is not as "good" as Handelsbanken in terms of impairment and credit risk, but it's still very solid. The company has a total credit impairment of 289M SEK for the quarter, with a risk exposure amount, or REA, of less than 850B SEK.

In terms of fundamentals and quality, Swedbank's private mortgage portfolio looks very good. 96% of the portfolio is now below a 70% LTV level, which might sound high in some geographies - but Swedes and Sweden have more loans and higher mortgages than is typical due to a number of factors. Credit impairments in terms of ratio remain close to an all-time low, take a look.

We're talking, in total here, of a credit impairment ratio of less than 0.2%, with an accumulated credit impairment since the early 1980s of 2.2B SEK. There has been a slight increase in past-due loans on the higher than 60-day distribution, up from lows of 0.1% to 0.13%, but it still marks a low point. Still, I will make a point of stating that many Swedes are now feeling the pain of overleveraging themselves and their homes - buying homes they could not afford outside ZIRP or even homes they don't want to afford when 70-80% of the disposable income goes to home payments and home-related bills. To call things perfect or even good is going too far - but Swedbank is nonetheless in a good position overall.

Swedbank also has very solid liquidity reserves and other related fundamentals. It's more than just the fact that the company has an A+ credit rating, a TEV of close to 1T SEK, or a market cap of close to a quarter trillion SEK. The company's liquidity coverage ratios, funding ratios, and survival horizon even if something were to happen is very solid, ergo; the bank we're talking about here is extremely stable and solid, even with its somewhat sordid recent past.

Let me show you in valuation what we can expect from Swedbank.

Swedbank - Not thrilled to invest here - but if it drops to 200 SEK, we can get 15% annualized

As I've said repeatedly before, my thesis and investment criteria for any company are centered around a 15% annualized conservative rate of return. For Swedbank, this calls for a 15% annualized dividend-inclusive RoR at the company's conservative P/E for either long or short-term, depending on what I believe to be likely for the company to be able to achieve.

In this case, I try both the long and short term. For the long term, the P/E is 10.41x. In this case, the company currently fulfills this. At 10.4x, we're seeing 18.34% RoR based on a 10.41x p/E, and this is with an annualized average EPS decline of 5.84%. Because it's an EPS decline we're talking about, and because I believe this decline to be quite likely though, I'm not all that interested in "going for" this going with the long-term premium for the business.

Even with the 7.34% yield (closer to 9.5% for me and my cost basis), we're not seeing a compelling upside at the 5-year basis, which I consider to be far more likely here given the declines, not only in 2024E but in 2025E (Source: FAST Graphs paywalled Link).

The 5-year average is closer to 9.3x. At that valuation, we find no more than a 13% annualized, which is below my target. If we go even shorter term, we find averages of between 7-8x, which go into the double-digit potential upsides, making it essentially entirely dividend-based.

And while this is an income investment for me, I do want capital appreciation potential when I go for things like this. For that reason, I do not consider the potential here all that compelling.

Swedbank's relevant ADR is SWDBY. This is an ORD 1x ADR, meaning we can translate it directly. It has very similar trends, and forecasting at the same rates, we also get sub-15% annualized RoR. In my latest article, I gave the company a PT of no more than 195 SEK per share. I believe strongly that this target has even more relevance these days than during my last article. This translates to 18.67 USD per share for the SWDBY ADR, and this is also where my PT ends up.

Do other analysts agree?

Sort of. Looking at S&P Global averages for this company, we find an average of 234 SEK, which initially sounds like most analysts think that this company should be bought here. Indeed, we're talking about a target range where my PT is actually the lowest-range target for Swedbank, of 195 SEK. However, out of 17 analysts, only 4 are actually at a "BUY". It's one of those classic situations where there is a clear disconnect between targets and recommendations. In fact, compared to a year ago, less than half of the analysts consider it to be a "Buy" or an outperform rating here, with a vast majority (12) either a "Hold", underperform or even "Sell" at 207 SEK/share.

For that reason, I consider my thesis clear and my recommendations logical. I don't see 3Q24 as being likely to change this. It'll be the latest quarter in a series of downturns as the interest rates go down. For that reason, I say that the thesis for the company is as follows here.

Thesis

  • Swedbank isn't exactly massively overvalued here, but it's not cheap either and lacks a compelling upside. I believe the operating results will deteriorate slightly over the next few years as the market worsens.
  • Given this potential pressure but also some potential advantages, I rate Swedbank conservatively and would "HOLD" the company at this valuation - but only insofar as other alternatives are attractive at this time.
  • I consider it time to raise my PT on Swedbank given what we're seeing here and what may happen in the coming year.
  • I give Swedbank a target of 195 SEK here, which is above my previous one, but it's below the current share price for the bank. That means Swedbank is a "HOLD", and I am keeping my rating for the bank here. This is updated as of October 2024.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

I will say that the company has an upside, but I will also say that the upside isn't as big as some others available here. Given what else is available on the market, I consider the company a "HOLD" here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Update On SPPP As Platinum And Palladium Prices Stabilize
data.description:
While the composite of the four precious metals traded on the CME's NYMEX and COMEX division was 11.60% higher over the first three quarters of 2024, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) were the two metals posting gains. Silver led the way on the upside with a 30.6% gain, and gold was 27.22% higher over the first nine months of this year. Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) and palladium (XPDUSD:CUR), industrial and precious metals, declined 1.6% and 9.8% over the period, with both prices closing Q3 around the $1,000 per ounce level.

Platinum and palladium move into Q4 in mostly bearish trends, but given the price action in gold and silver, their precious cousins, they offer value in the sector.

The Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (NYSEARCA:SPPP) owns physical platinum and palladium bullion. An early July Seeking Alpha article highlighted that platinum and palladium remained under pressure, concluding:

SPPP is under half the level at the 2021 and 2022 highs and could be on sale at under $10 per share. The physical ETF is an excellent tool for exposure to the leading platinum group metals that could benefit from limited liquidity over the coming years.

SPPP was at the $9.95 per share level on July 2, and the ETF was only marginally higher in late October at just over the $10 per share level. SPPP continues to offer value in the precious metals sector.

Platinum and palladium consolidating around the $1,000 per ounce level

Nearby NYMEX platinum and palladium futures were 2.3% lower and 2.3% higher, respectively, with prices around the $1,000 per ounce level on September 30, 2024. The platinum futures settled at $979, and palladium was $1,000.20 per ounce at the end of Q3.

The monthly NYMEX platinum futures chart illustrates the $1,000 per ounce pivot point over the past years.

The monthly NYMEX palladium futures chart highlights the decline from the March 2022 high and the recent trading pattern around the $1,000 per ounce level. In mid-October, January platinum futures were trading at $1,007.80, and December palladium futures were at $1,019.00 per ounce.

Gold and silver roar higher

While palladium and platinum futures were in sideways patterns around the $1,000 level, gold and silver prices were in bullish trends.

After reaching a record peak above $2,700 per ounce in September 2024, gold futures for December delivery were over $2,685 in mid-October.

The twenty-year silver chart highlights the marginal new multi-year high in October 2024, with silver just below $31.90 per ounce. A gravitational pull could cause platinum and palladium to rise as precious metals traders, speculators, and investors seek value-added alternatives.

Lower U.S. interest rates are not bearish for the Platinum Group Metals

The Fed's FOMC cut the short-term Fed Funds Rate by 50 basis points at the September meeting. The move was the first rate cut after the central bank raised rates from zero to a midpoint of 5.375% starting in 2022. The Fed indicated that rates may decline by another 50 basis points before the end of 2024, with the dot plot set at another 100 basis points of rate cuts in 2025.

For two reasons, lower interest rates are bullish for platinum, palladium, and all commodities. First, they lower the cost of carrying inventories. Second, since interest rate differentials are the most significant factor for the path of least resistance of currencies, falling U.S. interest rates could weigh on the U.S. dollar's value. A weaker dollar tends to support higher commodity prices, and platinum and palladium are no exceptions.

Liquidity issues could lead to rallies

Gold and silver are highly liquid futures markets, meaning daily volume and open interest, the total number of open long and short positions, support smooth trading and dampen price variance.

Platinum and palladium futures are far less liquid than gold and silver, experiencing lower daily volumes and open interest levels.

Less liquidity tends to lead to higher price variance, as bids to buy can disappear during downside trends and offers to sell can evaporate during price rallies. After years of sideways trading in the platinum futures arena and the downside plunge in palladium, which found the most recent bottom at just over $800 per ounce, the current consolidation could lead to recoveries, exacerbated by low liquidity.

SPPP still offers significant value

While gold and silver continue to rally, and the bullish trends are our best friends, platinum, and palladium could offer significant value at current prices. The top holdings of the Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust include:

As the chart shows, SPPP has approximately equal exposure to physical platinum and palladium. At around $10 per share, SPPP had around $150.64 million in assets under management. According to the Sprott.com website, the ETF charges a 1.17% management fee. SPPP trades an average of 190,859 shares daily.

Platinum and palladium prices have traded on either side of the $1,000 per ounce level in consolidation ranges.

The three-year chart illustrates the SPPP ETF consolidated at around $10 per share in 2024.

Adding SPPP to portfolios is a value approach to precious metals that could pay off over the coming months and years if the currency consolidation gives way to bullish trends in the thinly traded precious metals.

data.title: PTC Is Temporarily Overvalued, Despite A Strong SaaS Transition
data.description:
PTC Inc. (NASDAQ:PTC) is developing a strong annual recurring revenue model through its SaaS transition. While this should develop the company's cash flows substantially in the long term, it could cause moments of contraction along the way, as reported in the recent Q3 results. With a currently rich valuation, I expect to see a near-term contraction in its valuation multiples, even though I estimate it could achieve non-GAAP earnings per share of $6 in FY25, up from $5 in FY24. Based on my outlook, the stock is likely to increase to only $192 in FY25.

Operational Analysis

PTC is a prominent software and services company offering product lifecycle management ('PLM'), computer-aided design ('CAD') (including its flagship product Creo), Internet of Things ('IoT') (with a platform called ThingWorx), and augmented reality for product visualization and interaction. The company serves a variety of industries, including aerospace and defense, life sciences, electronics and high-tech, industrial machinery, and automotive sectors.

As management has built a business centered on digital transformation, the company is well-positioned given the macro trends with advanced analytics, design, manufacturing, and maintenance processes. Additionally, PTC's collaborations with Big Tech companies like AWS (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) significantly enhance its cloud-based offerings and expand its reach in the industrial IoT field. These partnerships and their established reputation help the business maintain a strong position with Fortune 500 companies who seek its services, particularly regarding manufacturing.

Despite a strong position in the industry, it is saturated with competition. Its most notable direct competitors include Autodesk (ADSK), Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), and Dassault Systèmes (OTCPK:DASTY).

  • Autodesk is known for its CAD software solutions like AutoCAD and Fusion 360. It is particularly strong in terms of cloud-based solutions, offering a flexible subscription model.
  • Siemens Digital Industries offers a range of industrial software solutions, including PLM. It offers market-leading digital twins integrating mechanical design, electrical systems, software, and multiphysics simulation; this gives it a competitive advantage.
  • Dassault Systèmes provides PLM solutions such as ENOVIA and 3DEXPERIENCE, the latter of which positions it strongly in terms of collaborative capabilities, offering a distinct unified environment for product innovation.

The majority of PTC's revenue still comes from on-premises solutions over Software-as-a-Service ('SaaS') cloud-based subscriptions. The company is attempting to significantly rewrite parts of its software stack, including Creo and Windchill, to fit a modern SaaS architecture based on its Atlas platform. This should position the company more formidably against its peer Autodesk, as well as keep it more broadly relevant. Moreover, a heavier SaaS approach will help the company to maintain its revenues through a recurring revenue model. I'm long-term bullish on SaaS and recurring revenue facilitated by cloud operations because subscription prices can also be refined and reweighted according to macroeconomic trends like inflation and interest rates. Therefore, it's a crucial strategy for cash flow sustainability.

Financial & Valuation Analysis

Full-year results are approaching on October 30th, and management has guided for year-over-year revenue growth of 8% to 11%, free cash flow year-over-year growth of around 23%, and non-GAAP earnings per share year-over-year growth of 12% to 20%. For the fourth quarter, management has guided for $598 million to $648 million in revenue and non-GAAP earnings per share of between $1.30 and $1.66.

Management mentioned in the Q3 earnings call that it has been facing challenges with close rates (an issue that has been going on for years) but also stated that broader macroeconomic factors (which I would largely put down to negative factors following the COVID-19 pandemic) are the key reason for this. We've seen a 50bps interest rate cut in September, but this isn't enough or long enough to change this outlook. Based on my analysis, lower close rates could be an ongoing issue for PTC, especially as only approximately 15% of its revenue comes from non-Western countries, and the West is currently vulnerable to stagflation and recession. That said, with a strong pipeline of deals and management's focus on efficiency recently (highlighted by the COO role being eliminated for a more hands-on approach from CEO Neil Barua), I think Q4 non-GAAP earnings per share will come in at approximately $1.50, with total revenues for the quarter at $610 million.

Looking more broadly, compared to its peers, PTC is richly valued. This is not helped by the fact that it has a low $247.7 million in cash and short-term investments and long-term debt of $1.29 billion as of the last report. In addition, PTC has contracting growth rates, with forward revenue growth down 11.7% from its five-year average. Moreover, its forward EBITDA growth is down 29.8% from its five-year average.

Based on these factors, I do not consider PTC to be the greatest investment at this time, primarily related to its valuation. That being said, I do consider it operationally strong, and I believe 2025 should be a strong year for the company, given the more favorable interest rate environment we are likely to see. Due to stronger macroeconomic conditions and PTC's continued focus on recurring revenue from its SaaS transition, I believe we could see non-GAAP earnings per share of $6 for full-year FY25. Based on this strong outlook, I expect only a moderate contraction in its P/E non-GAAP ratio (which is currently 9.4% higher than its five-year average). At a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 32 in FY25, the stock will trade at $192. Therefore, my rating is a Hold, especially as PTC does not pay a dividend.

Risk Analysis

The company's transition to SaaS has led to shorter contract terms for on-premises subscriptions, resulting in a temporary decrease in revenue (indicated by the 4.4% year-over-year decline in revenues in Q3). I expect this will be an ongoing challenge, though I do foresee that the transition will be worthwhile over the long term as investors will benefit from more stability, as I outlined above. However, given the costs and contraction risks associated with the SaaS transition, the stock's valuation is quite vulnerable right now. Any significant revenue declines as a result of failed integrations of newly acquired companies or market adoption challenges mean investors will be met with stock price volatility. That being said, as long as the operational challenges are not too severe, I will be considering a lower valuation as a potential buying opportunity.

Investors may wonder whether there is a threat of emerging technology outpacing SaaS and cloud services; there is no indication at the moment of any novel ecosystem development making PTC's current investments redundant. Technologies like edge computing and decentralized applications are more likely to complement cloud services rather than make them redundant. The only real concern is that SaaS platforms introduce new security risks, and therefore, PTC needs to keep this at the forefront of its mind. Any failure to adequately protect its systems from being hacked would have severe consequences for shareholders. This risk is intensified with cloud operations as hackers do not need access to on-premises hardware.

Conclusion

Based on my analysis, PTC is a strong company, but its valuation and near-term growth potential mean that this is not currently a Buy. Instead, my Hold rating comes with a caveat that if the stock's valuation becomes more favorable later on (potentially as a result of temporarily lower revenues due to the SaaS transition), this could open up a buying opportunity. Over the medium term to long term, a recurring revenue model is likely to be very beneficial for shareholders in terms of providing growth and cash flow stability. Therefore, PTC is a company worthy of going on watchlists.

data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the consumer staples sector. It is also a review of sector ETFs like The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: TransMedics Group: This Could Be One Of The Biggest Moats In A Decade
data.description:
The TransMedics Group Investment Thesis

I first became aware of TransMedics Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:TMDX) through social media earlier this year and purchased my first shares in April of this year. While this was a very small position for me, less than 1%, it is now a much larger part due to me adding shares and the favorable rise of the stock price.

What fascinated me about this company is that, in my opinion, they have an incredible product that helps to do something good, namely to extend or save lives. And I believe that they will continue to revolutionize the transplantation industry, and that is why I believe, as I will show later, that there is still a lot of upside potential and that TransMedics is a long-term buy and hold investment that I expect to do well over the next decade.

Business Model And Competition Position

Currently, TransMedics is the only FDA approved multi-organ platform with its three current offerings, OCS Lung, OCS Heart and OCS Liver. What makes TransMedics unique is that they have been able to keep organs alive longer than traditional methods, allowing the organs to travel greater distances. And because TransMedic's technology platform is designed to mimic the human body, it increases the number of organs that can actually be used.

If the organs are stored on ice and have no blood supply, this can lead to organ damage, and since the organs are not active during this time, it is not known whether they are damaged. So TransMedics has solved this problem and created an incredible competitive advantage, as clinical trials are very time-consuming and costly.

Currently, TransMedics is in the process of establishing a network of donor and transplant centers with which it will work on a long-term basis. This network could also be an important asset in the future to create further strong barriers to entry in addition to the technological lead.

In addition, it appears that TransMedics, through its acquired aviation division, is attempting to serve as the sole link between donor and transplant centers. This, in turn, is another step toward a stronger competitive advantage, as it means lower costs for customers, but also for TransMedics in the long run. A win-win situation.

By having its own fleet, the aircraft could soon be on standby 24 hours a day instead of just 12 hours, which is also positive for everyone involved. Especially when combined with the longer lifespan of donor organs, this results in fewer spontaneous night surgeries where the medical staff may not be 100% fit. Because anything that eliminates potential sources of error in the medical field will likely have a positive impact on surgical outcomes.

In my opinion, the more affordable transplantation becomes and the more healthy donor organs are available, the more transplantations will take place. Especially as the world's population continues to grow, and life expectancy continues to increase, the demand for transplants will also rise and is expected to lead to high growth rates. It is possible that TransMedics innovative product will even help fuel this growth.

In addition, there is a possibility that TransMedics will capture the kidney transplant market, which currently has the longest waiting list and where most transplants are performed. Plus, the aviation business could be growing faster than expected and TransMedics could be expanding internationally.

So I think TransMedics has great growth opportunities and is at a crossroads where they are laying the groundwork for the next decade. Another milestone is the inclusion of TransMedics in the S&P 600, which will lead to further inflows.

Valuation

Before we can start the valuation process, we need to take a look at the most recent earnings to get a better picture of the company. And here we can clearly see that the service or logistics segment has made a big jump due to the aircraft fleet. Unfortunately, this has also had an impact on margins, as service costs are significantly higher and I expect them to remain so for the next few years.

I am using 2028 as the endpoint of my valuation, as management has set a target of 10,000 transplants for FY2028. And I think that target should be achievable because it seems to me that management tends to be conservative, and therefore guidance tends to be set to be achievable.

So if TransMedics achieves 10,000 transplants at an average price of $105,000, they will achieve $1.05 billion in revenue, plus additional revenue, which I estimate at about $300 million. This would result in total revenues of $1.35 billion for FY28. As a net income margin, I use 15% because it gives me a margin of safety, but I think there is upside potential here because a higher net income margin is not out of the question.

My assumption is based on the following quote from the Medicare Payment Advisory Commission:

Large medical device companies are consistently profitable and typically have profit margins of 20 percent to 30 percent.

Therefore, I believe that the 15% margin is a conservative initial assumption when companies of similar quality in this industry typically range between 20% and 30%. And because TransMedics is still in the growth phase, I think it will take some time for them to reach the margin levels of the established players. But the potential is there.

The shares outstanding part is tricky. I am very confident that the shares outstanding in 2028 will be higher than they are now, but how much shareholders will be diluted is hard to estimate. But I think 40 million shares outstanding or maybe a little more is realistic.

Medical technology companies with a strong competitive advantage often have high multiples. And I definitely see TransMedics in the same boat as the peer group in the chart above in terms of quality of competitive advantage. TransMedics also has the advantage of being at the beginning of its journey, so its revenues are likely to continue to grow strongly through 2028, while the peer group is more likely to grow in the single digits.

Therefore, I believe the combination of TransMedics' near-monopoly and strong double-digit revenue growth justifies a 50x multiple. Intuitive Surgical, for example, had a multiple of 50x in 2009 and now, 15 years later, has a multiple of 83x. This means that this sector has the potential to reward high-quality companies with very high multiples over a longer period of time, in my opinion.

Revenue TTM $358m
Revenue 2028 $1.35b
Net Income (15% Margin) $202.5m
Shares Outstanding 40m
EPS $5.06
Multiple 50x
Share Price $253
Upside ~89%

With all this data, I have come to the conclusion that the stock has ~89% upside potential by the end of 2028. But I also think that my assumptions are quite conservative because the aviation part of the business could contribute a lot more to the total revenues, depending on how the fleet is expanded, and also because the net income margin could be more in the 20% range.

But even with my conservative assumptions, TransMedics looks like a compelling investment. And companies like Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) have shown that medical device companies that have a huge competitive advantage can grow strongly for years and trade at very high multiples for years.

Balance Sheet

Since TransMedics has only recently become profitable, my usual approach of comparing net income to long-term debt is not the right one for TransMedics. However, long term debt of nearly $507 million is countered by cash of $362 million. In addition, Q2/24 was the first positive FCF quarter with an excess of $2 million, and I expect FCF and interest coverage to grow rapidly in the coming months.

The liquidity situation seems to be in order, but is of course not as secure as with an established company that has been generating positive cash flows for years. So I think there is a little bit of risk there.

Risks

The above-mentioned liquidity situation, which offers a relatively low risk, can be added to the risk that competitors such as Xvivo (OTCPK:XVIPF), Organox or Lung Bioengineering could launch a similarly strong product. But even this risk I think is rather low at the moment because, as I said, clinical trials take a long time, and they would have to show better or similar results and those are not available yet.

Even xenotransplantation seems to be a long way off, as there have been first attempts, such as with David Bennett in 2022 and Richard Slayman this year, but the technology does not seem to be mature yet. But even if xenotransplantation continues to advance, opportunities for TransMedics could arise because here, too, it will be important to provide intact organs for as long and as well cooled as possible. Furthermore, I assume that most people would still prefer a human donor organ.

Conclusion

For me, TransMedics is a long-term buy-and-hold investment that I will probably hold for the next 10+ years. With such a long-time horizon, the competitive advantages are more important than the current multiple. But of course, I understand anyone with a shorter time horizon who says the company looks expensive right now because I also expect the price to be very volatile over the next few years.

But in conclusion, why I think TransMedics is attractive:

  • They operate in a market that they have revolutionized with their invention, creating strong competitive advantages and barriers to entry.
  • By becoming a trusted partner to donor and transplant centers, they can also build strong relationships and develop pricing power.
  • And a really big point is that this product really helps humanity.

data.title: BOE: Discount Opportunity Providing Global Exposure
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (NYSE:BOE) is a closed-end fund providing investors with a portfolio with a tilt toward global exposure. The fund also incorporates a covered call writing strategy on a portion of its underlying individual positions. This helps to generate cash flow to provide an attractive monthly distribution to its investors.

It has been just over a year since our last update, and the fund has delivered some attractive total returns. That said, it has lagged when compared to the results of the S&P 500 Index. The S&P 500 isn't necessarily the appropriate benchmark due to the fund's global and covered call strategy, but it can provide some overall context.

That was despite the discount having narrowed a bit since this update as well, which would have been a positive contributor to the total returns. I think the fund's discount is still wide enough to be of interest, especially with the discount management program that was announced earlier this year.

BOE Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 1.88.
  • Discount/Premium: -11.69%.
  • Distribution Yield: 6.71%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.09%.
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $764.7 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

BOE has an investment objective to "provide current income and current gains, with a secondary objective of long-term capital appreciation."

The fund intends to achieve this through the following:

...investing in at least 80% of its net assets in dividend-paying equity securities and at least 40% of its assets outside of the U.S. The Fund may invest in securities of companies of any market capitalization but intends to invest primarily in securities of large-capitalization companies. The Fund generally intends to write covered put and call options with respect to approximately 30% to 45% of its total assets. However, the percentage may vary from time to time with market conditions.

Performance - Attractive Discount Leads To Opportunity With Tender Offers

Earlier this year, the fund conducted a tender offer that was part of the fund's discount management program; with the next measurement period wrapping up now, the fund is set to conduct another tender offer, which is expiring for BOE on November 19, 2024.

The tender offer is triggered in any measurement period where the fund's discount averages -7.50% or wider. For BOE, that has been the case during this last measurement period and really or most of the last 10 years. There were only a couple of times that the fund trended toward a ~5% discount level but then quickly retreated lower.

This is a small offer at 2.5% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. However, as was the case with almost every tender offer, not everyone participates. That ends up seeing more than the 2.5% being accepted for the tender for investors who do participate.

In the case of BOE, the fund ended up accepting ~10.3% of investors' shares who elected to participate with 100% of their position. At 4x the 2.5% tender offer, that was a pretty solid outcome. The question now, after seeing several of the tender offers having significantly more shares accepted, is whether more investors will participate in the next round or not.

Historically speaking, the fund's performance hasn't been that great relative to funds that focus solely on U.S.-based investments. This has been an issue for a number of globally focused funds, as the U.S. equity space has been the clear outperformer for an extended period of time now.

However, while that has been the case for nearly two decades now, it has come with a cost more recently. That cost is the valuations in the U.S. have become quite elevated relative to their international counterparts. Should valuations start to matter again, or if the U.S. economy starts to slip up, we could be set up to underperform.

With having some added global exposure, it adds further diversification to one's portfolio. Another point to consider with BOE is that it isn't as heavily invested in the mega-cap tech names and there is more of a balance to its sector exposure. The fund still leaves heaviest in a tech allocation at 21.5%, but that can be compared to the S&P 500, which has seen its tech allocation climb to over 30% now.

With that said, the fund uses a benchmark of the MSCI ACWI and MSCI ACWI Call Overwrite Index, to help provide a better comparison. The MSCI ACWI Call Overwrite Index incorporates a 45% overwrite allocation. Unfortunately, BOE hasn't been able to keep pace with its benchmark historically, this can be the result of having operating expenses and also portfolio positioning.

Distribution - Could See A Boost

One area that negatively impacted the fund also being allocated to international investments that dragged down its relative performance was in the fund's distribution. The performance matters in terms of not only delivering dividends that can get passed onto shareholders, but as an equity fund, it will rely heavily on capital gains as well. That will either require appreciation from the underlying portfolio or collecting option premium from writing calls.

With weaker relative results, that meant a distribution that couldn't necessarily be supported without eroding NAV. That ultimately resulted in the fund cutting its distribution several times since its inception.

However, there is some optimism here as well, as I think they could use a distribution lift. Again, if international equities can start performing better, that means more fuel to fund the monthly distribution for investors.

Another sign though, that the distribution could use a boost is the actual distribution yield currently. Thanks to a hefty discount, the distribution rate on the share price comes to 6.71%. On an NAV basis, however, the distribution rate comes to an even more modest 5.93%. Over the last decade, that's what the fund has been able to deliver in terms of total NAV return results. Over the previous three and five-year periods annualized, the fund has been able to deliver beyond that rate by a fairly wide margin.

Of course, at the same time, whatever they don't pay out will simply go to the fund's NAV per share, and investors could potentially see appreciation. Looking at the latest semi-annual report, we can see exactly what the fund has been earning in terms of NII, and net realized/unrealized gains over the last several years. NII coverage in the latest report against the current monthly distribution works out to around 32%. For an equity fund, that isn't too unusual.

For tax purposes, the fund had seen a lot of its 2023 distribution classified as return of capital. We know this wasn't destructive to ROC as the NAV rose during the year, but they didn't realize a lot of those gains. In fact, they didn't realize any appreciation as they had a net realized loss for the year.

However, the year prior, they did sustain some significant losses in 2022. As of the end of 2023, the fund was still sitting on almost $25.4 million in capital loss carryforwards. That means we could see more ROC distributions this year as well, despite the NAV also climbing.

The remainder of the 2023 distribution was characterized as qualified dividend income. In general, the fund should see its distribution primarily as qualified dividend income and a return to long-term capital gains, assuming the fund continues to perform well. This would make it more appropriate for a taxable account as these classifications often see favorable tax-friendly treatments relative to ordinary income rates.

BOE's Portfolio

BOE delivers a global tilt, but it isn't entirely invested outside of the U.S. The U.S. alone still contributes to the largest single-country exposure, which is followed by primarily European countries after that. That includes the U.K., France, Switzerland, and Denmark as some larger weightings.

As noted above, the fund also isn't overly dominated by tech exposure-it is the largest exposure. Still, it isn't as weighty relative to what it has become for the broader market.

With broader market participation coming in over the last few months, we've seen that play out favorably for BOE. While the S&P 500 Index isn't the appropriate benchmark for a global fund that also utilizes covered calls, it can still provide some relative context.

Despite it being over a year since we last covered this fund, the actual portfolio sector allocations haven't changed too dramatically either. July 30 of last year saw information technology's weight at 19.06%, healthcare has slid lower some as it was previously the second-largest weighting at 18.85%. That is fairly material on a relative basis, but on an absolute basis, it hasn't been that much of a change. Financials were at 16.90% and industrials were at 14.45% at the time for comparison as well.

Several of the fund's largest holdings are also going to be familiar as being the same as last year as well, including Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Accenture plc (ACN), AstraZeneca PLC (AZN), Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). Positions such as MSFT and AAPL are pretty much staples in almost any diversified equity CEF, as these are part of the Magnificent 7 names.

For 2023, the turnover rate came to 46%, which indicates that they are fairly active in their management. In the last semi-annual report, turnover came in at 28%, putting it on pace to be above last year's should the second half reflect the first.

Conclusion

BOE trades at a deep discount, and that has historically been the case. In fact, the current discount is right around the fund's decade-long average. However, what is different now is that the fund has implemented a discount management program where investors can participate in tender offers at 98% of NAV. They are offering to repurchase 2.5% of shares, which is quite small, but there are four measurement periods. Further, not every investor will participate, and we saw that over 10% of shares were accepted by investors who did participate with their full position.

With that said, while that provides a potential upside catalyst, it isn't risk-free. If investors jump in and buy a position now, and we enter into a correction, those upside gains would be reduced or even eliminated, resulting in a loss for investors participating. At the end of the day, one shouldn't be buying a position just to participate in the tender offer but because they want exposure to the types of underlying assets that BOE provides for investors.

data.title: Early Earnings Calls Indicate A Resilient U.S. Economy
data.description:
Earnings season has just begun, with 6% of the companies in the S&P 500 Index having reported earnings so far. S&P 500 earnings growth for the third quarter is currently expected to be 4.1%. However, there are significant differences between sectors - technology earnings growth expectations are 14.9%, while earnings growth for the materials and energy sectors are forecast to be negative.1 In addition to the financial data being reported, the transcripts from earnings calls are giving us some interesting insights into the economy as corporate executives share their observations and outlooks.

It's important to note that most S&P 500 companies have beaten expectations in previous quarters. In fact, the actual earnings growth rate for the index has exceeded the estimated earnings growth rate in 37 of the last 40 quarters.1 FactSet Research anticipates this will happen again this quarter, and that earnings growth will likely end up being around 7% year-over-year after actual earnings are reported for all index companies, well above the current estimate of 4.1%.1 This should be supportive of stocks.

Of course, I'm also interested in earnings season for what it can tell me about the strength of the economy (or lack thereof), risks to the economy, different sectors in the economy, different consumer segments, as well as the outlook for the near term. I went through the earnings call transcripts of a number of companies - both in the S&P 500 and outside it - that reported earnings last week. Here are some learnings from those calls:

  • The US economy remains in good shape, with a soft landing likely. As one major financial services company explained, "These results are consistent with a soft landing. That's pretty consistent with this kind of Goldilocks economic situation."2
  • In general, consumers are healthy. As the same major financial services company explained: "So overall, we see the spending patterns as being sort of solid, consistent with the narrative that the consumer is on solid footing and consistent with a strong labor market." 2 Another financial services company shared, "We continue to look for changes in consumer health, but we have not seen meaningful changes in trends when looking at delinquency statistics across our consumer credit portfolios."3 However, in general, consumers are continuing to be price conscious; several companies described them as "discerning"4 and other companies described them as seeking "value."5
  • Higher income consumers seem to still be powering the US economy. As a major airline carrier explained on its earnings call, "Consumers are continuing to prioritize premium experiences, and our core customer base is in a healthy financial position with travel remaining a top spend category."6 It seems that higher income consumers are compensating for weakness among lower income consumers. As explained on a financial services earnings call, "We continue to see more pronounced stress in certain customer segments with lower deposit and asset levels, where inflation has partially offset strong employment and wage growth."7 This makes sense, given that higher income consumers are a smaller portion of the population but are generally considered responsible for the majority of US consumer spending.
  • The labor market seems to be the key to the health of the consumer. As one financial services company articulated, "Overall, customers in our US consumer businesses continue to hold up relatively well, benefiting from a strong labor market and wage growth."8 Companies that have seen some consumer weakness attribute it to the labor market. One consumer beverage company explained, "...ongoing macroeconomic headwinds, particularly rising unemployment, have led to a recent deceleration in the rate of growth of consumer demand for our products..."9
  • Corporations are in good shape as well. As explained on a financial services company earnings call, "Company balance sheets are strong, contributing to both consumption and investment in the economy..."10 Companies seem to be feeling optimistic about next year. As the airline carrier explained, "Corporate travel continues to improve…"11 Their recent corporate survey showed 85% of respondents expect their travel spend to increase in 2025.11
  • We can't forget the economic risk created by a rise in trade wars. One industrial company shared that one reason for pressure on its margins was an increase in trade barriers, "…certain countries have raised import duties, lifting the cost to us of moving product across borders."12

All in all, these initial earnings calls have offered some confirmation of the resilience we've been seeing in recent US economic data - and a reminder of some of the risks.

More details emerge on China's stimulus plan

In other news, I've been talking about the great potential impact of the stimulus package Chinese policymakers announced several weeks ago. At a press conference over the weekend, we got more details from the Chinese Ministry of Finance about their plans to use the Ministry's balance sheet to support longer-term growth. While the specific amount of the new stimulus package wasn't provided, the plan calls for an increase in government debt to help local governments' balance sheets, re-capitalize state-owned banks, support the property market, and deliver financial support to groups of people in need. This is a comprehensive plan, and our initial take is that it's likely to benefit the economy and markets in the medium and longer term with the intention of making positive structural changes.

Looking ahead

This week will bring more earnings reports and some key data, including US retail sales and US industrial production - which mean more insight into the strength of the US economy. In addition, we'll get reports on Canada and UK inflation as well as eurozone economic sentiment among other data releases.

As I read the earnings call transcripts, I will be focused on any guidance around job cuts. As I mentioned above, it seems the strength of the US consumer going forward will be dictated by the strength of the labor market. Despite some recent positive economic data, I am ever more vigilant in looking for cracks in the US economy, the epicenter of which would likely be a weakening in the labor market. With initial US jobless claims spiking last week to the highest level in more than a year, I will be looking for any signs of weakening in the labor market - since it arguably holds the key to the health of the US consumer, which in turn holds the key to the health of the US economy

With contributions from David Chao
