data.title: Ultralife Corporation: A Battery Stock Charging Up For A Comeback
data.description:
Ultralife Corporation (NASDAQ:ULBI) is just a week from releasing Q3 earnings results, on October 24.

Considering the 25% decline since the release of Q2 results, investors are wondering if now is a good time to initiate a long position in this stock.

In my view, considering the cheap valuation ratios and the strong fundamentals of the company, accompanied by a jump in interest from institutional investors, I believe the upside outweighs the risks that the company is currently facing.

In the outlook section, I will provide the rationale behind my Buy rating in this stock.

For now, I would like to begin with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

Imagine you're out on a remote military mission. Your communication device needs to stay charged, your backup power system needs to be reliable, and the equipment you use to send signals has to work in all weather conditions. Ultralife Corporation is the company behind making sure all that happens.

This is a US-based company with a portfolio of products ranging from rechargeable and non-rechargeable batteries to charging systems, and communication electronics, serving commercial, government, and defense markets worldwide.

Therefore, the company has two main business segments: battery & energy, and communication systems. I considered including below a breakdown of the YTD revenue from their latest 10-Q.

Year Battery & Energy Products Communications Systems Total Revenue
2024 (YTD, June) $71.67M $13.24M $84.91M
2023 (YTD, June) $62.33M $12.28M $74.61M

As already mentioned, they operate internationally, so I included below a breakdown of YTD revenue by geographical area.

Year United States Revenue Non-United States Revenue Total Revenue
2024 (YTD, June) $47.86M $37.05M $84.91M
2023 (YTD, June) $37.98M $36.62M $74.61M

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows a beneficial ownership of 40% among all 6 directors and executive officers. I am highly encouraged about this figure, as I favor companies where management has skin in the game.

Headwinds, Risks, And Challenges

Let's start with dessert.

The communication systems segment is taking a big hit, with a 28.7% YoY revenue decline in this segment. The main driver was delays in shipments to defense contractors that were supposed to occur in earlier periods but were pushed back due to ongoing supply chain disruptions.

These orders were mainly vehicle amplifier adapters and radio vehicle mounts. Although management didn't provide full details during the earnings call, or in the Q2 earnings report, it seems (see section 2 of this report) that there are industry-wise shortages for semiconductors, electronic components, and raw materials including aluminum and copper, which are used in military communications systems.

It appears that the supply chain disruptions are external to the company, so I am not overly concerned about this issue.

However, the backlog decline is concerning. In Q2 2023, the company's backlog stood at $108 million. Today, it stands at $93 million, a 14% decline YoY. In my view, the delays in the communication systems segment could have played a big role in this decline, with defense contractors easing off new orders until the supply chain disruptions are sorted out.

Aside from the revenue decline in this segment, gross profit also declined by a significant amount. Specifically, in Q2 2024, the communication systems segment's gross profit declined by 890 bps YoY. The main driver was an unfavorable product mix, mainly due to these disruptions.

It appears that the vehicle amplifier adapters have high gross margins. Therefore, due to the delays in the supply chain, shipments of these high-margin products were replaced by lower-margin items, leading to a decline in gross margin.

Moving on to other pressures, oil and gas market sales experienced a 10.9% decline in Q2 2024. Management attributed this decline to overbuying activity in Q2 2023, which led to an inventory correction during the last quarter. Additionally, the volatility of the natural gas sector might have contributed to this revenue decline.

Finally, I see a moderate risk due to the high reliance on US government and defense contracts in the battery & energy segment. In Q2 2024, 30.5% of battery & energy Products sales growth was attributed to US government and defense contracts alone.

With the upcoming U.S. election, changes in government policies could affect the growth rate of this segment.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

As shown in the daily chart below, since May, the share price has declined by over 33%. Additionally, there appears to be an upward trend, although I don't consider the trend line to be a reliable support level.

If we compare the return of Ultralife to its sector, industry, and the S&P 500 over the past 2 years, it appears that returns are very volatile, with an outperformance in May this year, and underperformance during the first half of 2023.

So, considering the 33% decline since May, does it make sense to initiate a long position in this stock? In my view, the answer is yes, although my conviction is not very high. Therefore, I maintain a Buy rating, instead of a Strong Buy. Let me explain below why.

A quick look at the income statement shows consistent revenue growth YoY since March 2022.

Gross income and operating income growth follow. However, net income has declined by 11% in the last quarter.

Despite the 11% decline in net income, I am not worried, due to the fact that the company benefited in Q2 2023 from a $1.544 million Employee Retention Credit, which significantly boosted "other income" in the income statement.

As a side note, if you are wondering about the non-operating income from Q2 2024, Ultralife Corporation received $235,000 in insurance payments related to a ransomware cyberattack that occurred in Q1 2023.

Moving on to the balance sheet, there was a significant decline in net debt in the last quarter. The company reduced borrowings on its revolving credit facility by $12.68 million and made scheduled payments of $1 million on its term loan.

Additionally, I am encouraged by the improvement in tangible book value per share.

With regard to the debt structure, I have included below a summary using the latest earnings report.

Debt Type Amount Maturity Date Interest Rate Interest Type Annual Principal Payments
Term Loan Facility $5.167 million January 1, 2027 Daily Simple SOFR + 0.10% + 2.10-2.40% Variable $1 million
Revolving Credit Facility $6.902 million May 30, 2028 Daily Simple SOFR + 0.10% + 2.10-2.40% Variable N/A (interest-only until maturity)

As a side note, the revolving credit facility can be increased to $50 million up to six months before the maturity date.

Their liquidity ratios look really good, so I am not concerned with their ability to pay down debt.

Liquidity Ratio Value
Current Ratio 4.07
Quick Ratio 2.06
Cash Ratio 0.33

Moving now to the cash flow statement, I am encouraged by the improvement in the last quarter.

It appears that the company has finally managed to improve its working capital, with accounts receivables leading to a positive impact of $654k compared to the negative impact of $803k in H1 2023. Additionally, accounts payable and other liabilities decreased by $2.56 million, compared to a $413k increase in 2023. Finally, there was also an improvement in inventory management, with a positive cash inflow of $717k in H1 2024, compared to a $4.88 million cash outflow in the same period last year.

Moving on to insider buying activity, there were only two transactions in 2024, but they were very small in size, so they don't provide any relevant signal.

However, one of the activist investors, Whitmore Bradford, increased his ownership in the company by 7.2% in September this year. In my view, this is encouraging, considering that he bought on a day when shares declined by 7.7%. My investment style favors this type of contrarian buying activity.

A quick look at institutional investor activity shows an increase of 17.8% in the total shares reported in 13F filings and a 67% decline in the put/call ratio. In my view, there is a bullish sentiment among institutional investors, considering that the number of new positions during the last quarter increased by 42%.

Moving on to valuation, I believe the company is undervalued. A quick look at the football field chart below shows most of the valuation ratios trading close to the 5-year minimum.

Additionally, when comparing these ratios to some competitors' the shares look cheap. An example is shown below, where the table is sorted in descending order by P/TB value.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I believe Ultralife Corporation presents a compelling opportunity at its current valuation.

Over the last 2 years, the company has shown consistent revenue growth, particularly in the battery and energy products segment, which benefits from strong demand in both commercial and defense markets.

The significant improvement in working capital, debt reduction, and a strong liquidity position further strengthens my bull case. With institutional investors increasing their stakes and the stock trading near five-year valuation lows, I see potential for meaningful upside, particularly if they manage to mitigate the recent supply chain disruptions.

However, there are some risks to be aware of, particularly in the communication systems segment, where revenue declined by 28.7% in the last year. Additionally, the reliance on U.S. government contracts introduces a level of uncertainty, especially with potential changes in policy following upcoming elections.

To conclude, while Ultralife faces some headwinds, I believe the fundamentals and the cheap valuation ratios outweigh the risks.
